key_points: India's geographical location in South Asia provides it a strategic position in the Asian continent
The mainland of India extends from 8°4'N to 37°6'N latitudes and 68°7'E to 97°25'E longitudes
The Tropic of Cancer divides India into tropical and subtropical climatic zones influencing vegetation and climate
India's geographical area of about 3.28 million sq km makes it the seventh largest country in the world
India has a long land boundary of approximately 15,200 kilometres
The total coastline including island territories measures about 7,516.6 kilometres
India is surrounded by the Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal and Indian Ocean on three sides
The Standard Meridian of India (82°30'E) helps maintain a uniform time across the country
India's peninsular shape helped it develop maritime trade with distant regions
India's central location between East and West Asia made it an important centre of ancient trade
Mountain passes in the Himalayas allowed traders and travellers to enter India
Sea routes across the Indian Ocean connected India with Europe, Africa and Southeast Asia
Indian cultural ideas such as the decimal system spread through trade contacts
India's location contributed to the exchange of goods, ideas and cultural traditions
The island groups of India strengthen its maritime and strategic position
examples: Example of historical trade route: Sea routes connecting India with Europe
Example of cultural diffusion: Spread of Indian numerals and decimal system
Example of strategic island group: Andaman and Nicobar Islands near major sea routes
Example of coral islands: Lakshadweep Islands
Example of island neighbour: Sri Lanka separated by the Palk Strait
Example of maritime neighbour: Maldives located south of Lakshadweep
formulas: Area of India ≈ 3.28 million sq km
exam_tips: Explain the importance of India's central location in Asia with examples
Focus on trade routes and cultural exchanges in ancient times
Understand how India's peninsular shape influenced maritime trade
Remember the role of the Himalayas and oceans in shaping India's geography
Practice map work including neighbouring countries and island groups
